title: Sea level

infobox.value: (**MSL**, often shortened to **sea level**) is an [average](/wiki/Mean "Mean") surface level of one or more among [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")'s coastal [bodies of water](/wiki/Body_of_water "Body of water") from which heights such as [elevation](/wiki/Elevation "Elevation") may be measured. The global MSL is a type of [vertical datum](/wiki/Vertical_datum "Vertical datum") – a standardised [geodetic datum](/wiki/Geodetic_datum "Geodetic datum") – that is used, for example, as a [chart datum](/wiki/Chart_datum "Chart datum") in [cartography](/wiki/Cartography "Cartography") and [marine navigation](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation"), or, in aviation, as the [standard sea level](/wiki/Standard_sea_level "Standard sea level") at which [atmospheric pressure](/wiki/Atmospheric_pressure "Atmospheric pressure") is measured to [calibrate](/wiki/Calibration "Calibration") altitude and, consequently, aircraft [flight levels](/wiki/Flight_level "Flight level"). A common and relatively straightforward mean sea-level standard is instead a long-term average of [tide gauge](/wiki/Tide_gauge "Tide gauge") readings at a particular reference location.[[1]](#cite_note-1)
means above present sea level, comparing sea levels in the past with the level today.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Geographical reference point from which various heights are measured

For other uses, see [Sea level (disambiguation)](/wiki/Sea_level_(disambiguation) "Sea level (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Israel_Sea_Level_BW_1.JPG/500px-Israel_Sea_Level_BW_1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Israel_Sea_Level_BW_1.JPG)

This marker indicating sea level is situated between [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") and the [Dead Sea](/wiki/Dead_Sea "Dead Sea").

**Mean sea level** (**MSL**, often shortened to **sea level**) is an [average](/wiki/Mean "Mean") surface level of one or more among [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")'s coastal [bodies of water](/wiki/Body_of_water "Body of water") from which heights such as [elevation](/wiki/Elevation "Elevation") may be measured. The global MSL is a type of [vertical datum](/wiki/Vertical_datum "Vertical datum") – a standardised [geodetic datum](/wiki/Geodetic_datum "Geodetic datum") – that is used, for example, as a [chart datum](/wiki/Chart_datum "Chart datum") in [cartography](/wiki/Cartography "Cartography") and [marine navigation](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation"), or, in aviation, as the [standard sea level](/wiki/Standard_sea_level "Standard sea level") at which [atmospheric pressure](/wiki/Atmospheric_pressure "Atmospheric pressure") is measured to [calibrate](/wiki/Calibration "Calibration") altitude and, consequently, aircraft [flight levels](/wiki/Flight_level "Flight level"). A common and relatively straightforward mean sea-level standard is instead a long-term average of [tide gauge](/wiki/Tide_gauge "Tide gauge") readings at a particular reference location.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The term *above sea level* generally refers to the [height above mean sea level](/wiki/Height_above_mean_sea_level "Height above mean sea level") (AMSL). The term **APSL** means above present sea level, comparing sea levels in the past with the level today.

Earth's radius at sea level is 6,378.137 km (3,963.191 mi) at the equator. It is 6,356.752 km (3,949.903 mi) at the poles and 6,371.001 km (3,958.756 mi) on average.[[2]](#cite_note-2) This [flattened spheroid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid"), combined with local [gravity anomalies](/wiki/Gravity_anomalies "Gravity anomalies"), defines the [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid") of the Earth, which approximates the local mean sea level for locations in the open ocean. The geoid includes a significant [depression in the Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_Geoid_Low "Indian Ocean Geoid Low"), whose surface dips as much as 106 m (348 ft) below the global mean sea level (excluding minor effects such as tides and currents).[[3]](#cite_note-Geoid-3) It was found in 2026 that sea levels estimated using the geoid used in 90% of published work were about 25cm lower than measured values on average, hence underestimating the effect of sea level rise.

## Measurement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sea_level&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Measurement")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Recent_Sea_Level_Rise.png/250px-Recent_Sea_Level_Rise.png)](/wiki/File:Recent_Sea_Level_Rise.png)

Sea level measurements from 23 long [tide gauge](/wiki/Tide_gauge "Tide gauge") records in geologically stable environments show a rise of around 200 millimetres (7.9 in) during the 20th century (2 mm/year).

Precise determination of a "mean sea level" is difficult because of the many factors that affect sea level.[[4]](#cite_note-USNRC32-4) Instantaneous sea level varies substantially on several scales of time and space. This is because the sea is in constant motion, affected by the tides, tsunamis, [wind](/wiki/Wind "Wind"), atmospheric pressure, local gravitational differences, temperature, [salinity](/wiki/Salinity "Salinity"), and so forth. The mean sea level at a particular location may be calculated over an extended time period and used as a [datum](/wiki/Datum_(geodesy) "Datum (geodesy)"). For example, hourly measurements may be averaged over a full [Metonic 19-year lunar cycle](/wiki/Metonic_19-year_lunar_cycle "Metonic 19-year lunar cycle") to determine the mean sea level at an official [tide gauge](/wiki/Tide_gauge "Tide gauge").[[5]](#cite_note-Terms-5)

*Still-water level* or *still-water sea level* (SWL) is the level of the sea with motions such as [wind waves](/wiki/Wind_waves "Wind waves") averaged out.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
Then MSL implies the SWL further averaged over a period of time such that changes due to, e.g., the [tides](/wiki/Tide "Tide"), also have zero mean.
*Global MSL* refers to a spatial average over the entire ocean area, typically using large sets of tide gauges and/or satellite measurements.[[5]](#cite_note-Terms-5)

One often measures the values of MSL with respect to the land; hence a change in *relative MSL* or ([relative sea level](/wiki/Relative_sea_level "Relative sea level")) can result from a real change in sea level, or from a change in the height of the land on which the tide gauge operates, or both.
In the UK, the [ordnance datum](/wiki/Ordnance_Datum "Ordnance Datum") (the 0 metres height on UK maps) is the mean sea level measured at [Newlyn](/wiki/Newlyn "Newlyn") in Cornwall between 1915 and 1921.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Before 1921, the [vertical datum](/wiki/Vertical_datum "Vertical datum") was MSL at the [Victoria Dock, Liverpool](/wiki/Victoria_Dock_(Liverpool) "Victoria Dock (Liverpool)").
Since the times of the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), in [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") and its other former parts, now independent states, the sea level is measured from the zero level of [Kronstadt](/wiki/Kronstadt "Kronstadt") Sea-Gauge.
In Hong Kong, "mPD" is a surveying term meaning "metres above Principal Datum" and refers to height of 0.146 m (5.7 in) above [chart datum](/wiki/Chart_datum "Chart datum")[[8]](#cite_note-8) and 1.304 m (4 ft 3.3 in) below the average sea level.
In France, the Marégraphe in Marseille measures continuously the sea level since 1883 and offers the longest collated data about the sea level. It is used for a part of continental Europe and the main part of Africa as the official sea level. [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") uses the reference to measure heights below or above sea level at [Alicante](/wiki/Alicante "Alicante"), while the [European Vertical Reference System](/wiki/EUREF#European_Vertical_Reference_System_(EVRS) "EUREF") is calibrated to the [Amsterdam Peil](/wiki/Amsterdam_Ordnance_Datum "Amsterdam Ordnance Datum") elevation, which dates back to the 1690s.

Satellite altimeters have been making precise measurements of sea level since the launch of [TOPEX/Poseidon](/wiki/TOPEX/Poseidon "TOPEX/Poseidon") in 1992.[[9]](#cite_note-9) A joint mission of [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") and [CNES](/wiki/CNES "CNES"), TOPEX/Poseidon was followed by [Jason-1](/wiki/Jason-1 "Jason-1") in 2001 and the [Ocean Surface Topography Mission](/wiki/Ocean_Surface_Topography_Mission "Ocean Surface Topography Mission") on the Jason-2 satellite in 2008.

### Height above mean sea level

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sea_level&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Height above mean sea level")]

Main article: [Height above mean sea level](/wiki/Height_above_mean_sea_level "Height above mean sea level")

*Height above mean sea level* (*AMSL*) is the elevation (on the ground) or altitude (in the air) of an object, relative to a reference datum for mean sea level (MSL). It is also used in aviation, where some heights are recorded and reported with respect to mean sea level (contrast with [flight level](/wiki/Flight_level "Flight level")), and in the [atmospheric sciences](/wiki/Atmospheric_sciences "Atmospheric sciences"), and in [land surveying](/wiki/Land_surveying "Land surveying"). An alternative is to base height measurements on a [reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid") approximating the entire Earth, which is what systems such as [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS") do. In aviation, the reference ellipsoid known as [WGS84](/wiki/WGS84 "WGS84") is increasingly used to define heights; however, differences up to 100 metres (328 feet) exist between this ellipsoid height and local mean sea level.[[3]](#cite_note-Geoid-3) Another alternative is to use a [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid")-based vertical [datum](/wiki/Datum_(geodesy) "Datum (geodesy)") such as [NAVD88](/wiki/North_American_Vertical_Datum_of_1988 "North American Vertical Datum of 1988") and the global [EGM96](/wiki/EGM96 "EGM96") (part of WGS84). Details vary in different countries.

When referring to [geographic](/wiki/Geographic "Geographic") features such as mountains, on a [topographic map](/wiki/Topographic_map "Topographic map") variations in elevation are shown by [contour lines](/wiki/Contour_line "Contour line"). A mountain's highest point or summit is typically illustrated with the AMSL height in metres, feet or both. In unusual cases where a land location is below sea level, such as [Death Valley, California](/wiki/Death_Valley,_California "Death Valley, California"), the elevation AMSL is negative.

#### Difficulties in use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sea_level&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Difficulties in use")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Geoida.svg/250px-Geoida.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Geoida.svg)

1. Ocean
2. [Reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid")
3. Local [plumb line](/wiki/Plumb-bob "Plumb-bob")
4. [Continent](/wiki/Continent "Continent")
5. [Geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid")

It is often necessary to compare the local height of the mean sea surface with a "level" reference surface, or geodetic datum, called the [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid"). In the absence of external forces, the local mean sea level would coincide with this geoid surface, being an equipotential surface of the Earth's [gravitational](/wiki/Gravitation "Gravitation") field which, in itself, does not conform to a simple sphere or ellipsoid and exhibits [gravity anomalies](/wiki/Gravity_anomalies "Gravity anomalies") such as those measured by NASA's [GRACE satellites](/wiki/GRACE_satellites "GRACE satellites"). In reality, the geoid surface is not directly observed, even as a long-term average, due to ocean currents, air pressure variations, temperature and salinity variations, etc. The location-dependent but time-persistent separation between local mean sea level and the geoid is referred to as (mean) [ocean surface topography](/wiki/Ocean_surface_topography "Ocean surface topography"). It varies globally in a typical range of ±1 m (3 ft).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Work published in 2026 analysed 385 peer-reviewed scientific papers published since 2009 and compared the difference between calculations using commonly assumed coastal sea levels using global geoid models with actual measurements. It was found that over 90% of studies used land elevation measurements referenced against global geoid models instead of local direct measurements. This underestimated levels by an average of 24-27cm, with some discrepancies reaching 550-760cm, a discrepancy described as an "interdisciplinary blind spot". The effect of this underestimation is that for a sea level rise of 1m, 37% more coastal areas than previously estimated will fall below sea level, affecting up to 132 million people.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Dry land

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sea_level&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Dry land")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/BadwaterSL.JPG/250px-BadwaterSL.JPG)](/wiki/File:BadwaterSL.JPG)

Sea level sign seen on cliff (circled in red) at [Badwater Basin](/wiki/Badwater_Basin "Badwater Basin"), [Death Valley National Park](/wiki/Death_Valley_National_Park "Death Valley National Park")

Several terms are used to describe the changing relationships between sea level and dry land.

* "relative" means change relative to a fixed point in the sediment pile.[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* "eustatic" refers to global changes in sea level relative to a fixed point, such as the centre of the earth, for example as a result of melting ice-caps.[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* "steric" refers to global changes in sea level due to [thermal expansion](/wiki/Thermal_expansion "Thermal expansion") and [salinity](/wiki/Salinity "Salinity") variations.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* "isostatic" refers to changes in the level of the land relative to a fixed point in the earth, possibly due to thermal buoyancy or [tectonic](/wiki/Tectonics "Tectonics") effects, disregarding changes in the volume of water in the oceans.

The melting of [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") at the end of [ice ages](/wiki/Ice_age "Ice age") results in isostatic [post-glacial rebound](/wiki/Post-glacial_rebound "Post-glacial rebound"), when land rises after the weight of ice is removed. Conversely, older volcanic islands experience [relative sea level](/wiki/Relative_sea_level "Relative sea level") rise, due to isostatic [subsidence](/wiki/Subsidence "Subsidence") from the weight of cooling volcanos. The subsidence of land due to the withdrawal of [groundwater](/wiki/Groundwater "Groundwater") is another isostatic cause of relative sea level rise.

On planets that lack a liquid ocean, [planetologists](/wiki/Planetologist "Planetologist") can calculate a "mean altitude" by averaging the heights of all points on the surface. This altitude, sometimes referred to as a "sea level" or [zero-level elevation](/wiki/Zero-level_elevation "Zero-level elevation"), serves equivalently as a reference for the height of planetary features.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Change

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sea_level&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Change")]

See also: [Past sea level](/wiki/Past_sea_level "Past sea level") and [Sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise")

### Local and eustatic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sea_level&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Local and eustatic")]

See also: [Eustatic sea level](/wiki/Eustatic_sea_level "Eustatic sea level")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Mass_balance_atmospheric_circulation.png/250px-Mass_balance_atmospheric_circulation.png)](/wiki/File:Mass_balance_atmospheric_circulation.png)

[Water cycles](/wiki/Water_cycle "Water cycle") between ocean, [atmosphere](/wiki/Earth%27s_atmosphere "Earth's atmosphere") and [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier")

**Local mean sea level (LMSL)** is defined as the height of the sea with respect to a land benchmark, averaged over a period of time long enough that fluctuations caused by [waves](/wiki/Ocean_surface_wave "Ocean surface wave") and [tides](/wiki/Tide "Tide") are smoothed out, typically a year or more. One must adjust perceived changes in LMSL to account for vertical movements of the land, which can occur at rates similar to [sea level changes](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise") (millimetres per year).

Some land movements occur because of [isostatic](/wiki/Isostasy "Isostasy") adjustment to the melting of [ice sheets](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet") at the end of the [last ice age](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period"). The weight of the ice sheet depresses the underlying land, and when the ice melts away the [land slowly rebounds](/wiki/Post-glacial_rebound "Post-glacial rebound"). Changes in ground-based ice volume also affect local and regional sea levels by the readjustment of the [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid") and [true polar wander](/wiki/True_polar_wander "True polar wander"). [Atmospheric pressure](/wiki/Atmospheric_pressure "Atmospheric pressure"), [ocean currents](/wiki/Ocean_current "Ocean current") and local ocean temperature changes can affect LMSL as well.

**Eustatic sea level** change (global as opposed to local change) is due to change in either the volume of water in the world's oceans or the volume of the [oceanic basins](/wiki/Oceanic_basin "Oceanic basin").[[17]](#cite_note-Oilfield_Glossary-17) Two major mechanisms are currently causing eustatic sea level rise. First, shrinking land ice, such as mountain glaciers and polar ice sheets, is releasing water into the oceans. Second, as ocean temperatures rise, the warmer water expands.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Short-term and periodic changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sea_level&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Short-term and periodic changes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Global_sea_levels_during_the_last_Ice_Age.jpg/250px-Global_sea_levels_during_the_last_Ice_Age.jpg)](/wiki/File:Global_sea_levels_during_the_last_Ice_Age.jpg)

The [Last Glacial Period](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period") caused a much lower global sea level.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Glaciers_and_Sea_Level_Rise_%288742463970%29.jpg/250px-Glaciers_and_Sea_Level_Rise_%288742463970%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glaciers_and_Sea_Level_Rise_(8742463970).jpg)

Warming temperatures and melting glaciers are currently raising the sea level.

Many factors can produce short-term changes in sea level, typically within a few metres, in timeframes ranging from minutes to months:

| Periodic sea level changes | | |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Diurnal and semidiurnal astronomical tides | 12–24 h P | 0.1–10+ m |
| Long-period tides | 2-week to 1-year P | <0.1 m |
| Pole tides ([Chandler wobble](/wiki/Chandler_wobble "Chandler wobble")) | 14-month P | 5 mm |
| Meteorological and oceanographic fluctuations | | |
| Atmospheric pressure | Hours to months | −0.7 to 1.3 m |
| Winds ([storm surges](/wiki/Storm_surge "Storm surge")) | 1–5 days | Up to 5 m |
| [Evaporation](/wiki/Evaporation "Evaporation") and [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)") (may also follow long-term pattern) | Days to weeks | <0.1m |
| [Ocean surface topography](/wiki/Ocean_surface_topography "Ocean surface topography") (changes in water [density](/wiki/Density "Density") and currents) | Days to weeks | Up to 1 m |
| [El Niño](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o "El Niño")/[southern oscillation](/wiki/Southern_oscillation "Southern oscillation") | 6 mo every 5–10 yr | Up to 0.6 m |
| Seasonal variations | | |
| Seasonal water balance among oceans (Atlantic, Pacific, Indian) | 6 months |  |
| Seasonal variations in slope of water surface | 6 months |  |
| River runoff/floods | 2 months | 1 m |
| Seasonal water density changes (temperature and [salinity](/wiki/Salinity "Salinity")) | 6 months | 0.2 m |
| Seiches | | |
| [Seiches](/wiki/Seiche "Seiche") (standing waves) | Minutes to hours | Up to 2 m |
| Earthquakes | | |
| [Tsunamis](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami") (catastrophic long-period waves) | Hours | 0.1–10+ m |
| Abrupt change in land level | Minutes | Up to 10 m |

### Recent changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sea_level&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Recent changes")]

Further information: [Ocean heat content](/wiki/Ocean_heat_content "Ocean heat content") and [Effects of climate change on oceans](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change_on_oceans "Effects of climate change on oceans")

This section is an excerpt from [Sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Sea_level_rise&action=edit)]

The sea level has been rising since the end of the [Last Glacial Maximum](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Maximum "Last Glacial Maximum"), which was around 20,000 years ago.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Between 1901 and 2018, the average sea level rose by 15–25 cm (6–10 in), with an increase of 2.3 mm (0.091 in) per year since the 1970s.[[20]](#cite_note-Sea_level_rise_Fox-Kemper-2021-20): 1216 This was faster than the sea level had ever risen over at least the past 3,000 years.[[20]](#cite_note-Sea_level_rise_Fox-Kemper-2021-20): 1216 The rate accelerated to 4.62 mm (0.182 in)/yr for the decade 2013–2022.[[21]](#cite_note-Sea_level_rise_World_Meteorological_Organization-2023-21) [Climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") due to human activities is the main cause of this persistent acceleration.[[22]](#cite_note-Sea_level_rise_IPCC-22): 5, 8 Between 1993 and 2018, melting [ice sheets](/wiki/Ice_sheets "Ice sheets") and [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") accounted for 44% of sea level rise, with another 42% resulting from [thermal expansion](/wiki/Thermal_expansion "Thermal expansion") of [water](/wiki/Water "Water").[[23]](#cite_note-Sea_level_rise_WCRP_Global_Sea_Level_Budget_Group-2018-23): 1576

Sea level rise lags behind changes in the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")'s temperature by decades, and sea level rise will therefore continue to accelerate between now and 2050 in response to warming that has already happened.[[24]](#cite_note-Sea_level_rise_National_Academies_of_Sciences,_Engineering,_and_Medicine-2011-24) What happens after that depends on future human [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions"). If there are very deep cuts in emissions, sea level rise would slow between 2050 and 2100. The reported factors of increase in flood hazard potential are often exceedingly large, ranging from 10 to 1000 for even modest sea-level rise scenarios of 0.5 m or less.[[25]](#cite_note-25) It could then rise by between 30 cm (1 ft) and 1.0 m (3+1⁄3 ft) between the early 2020s and 2100, or by approximately 60 cm (2 ft) to 130 cm (4+1⁄2 ft) from the 19th century to 2100. With high emissions it would instead accelerate further, and could rise by 50 cm (1.6 ft) or even by 1.9 m (6.2 ft) by 2100.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[22]](#cite_note-Sea_level_rise_IPCC-22)[[20]](#cite_note-Sea_level_rise_Fox-Kemper-2021-20): 1302 In the long run, sea level rise would amount to 2–3 m (7–10 ft) over the next 2000 years if warming stays to its current 1.5 °C (2.7 °F) over the pre-industrial past. It would be 19–22 metres (62–72 ft) if warming peaks at 5 °C (9.0 °F).[[22]](#cite_note-Sea_level_rise_IPCC-22): 21

Rising seas affect every coastal population on Earth.[[27]](#cite_note-27) This can be through flooding, higher [storm surges](/wiki/Storm_surge "Storm surge"), [king tides](/wiki/King_tide "King tide"), and increased vulnerability to [tsunamis](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami"). There are many knock-on effects. They lead to loss of coastal [ecosystems](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem") such as [mangrove swamps](/wiki/Mangrove_swamp "Mangrove swamp"). There may be a reduction in [crop yields](/wiki/Crop_yield "Crop yield") because of [increasing salt levels](/wiki/Freshwater_salinization "Freshwater salinization") in [irrigation](/wiki/Irrigation "Irrigation") water. Damage to ports disrupts sea trade.[[28]](#cite_note-Sea_level_rise_International_Panel_on_Climate_Change,_Cambridge_University_Press-2001-28)[[29]](#cite_note-Sea_level_rise_Holder-2017-29) The sea level rise projected by 2050 will expose places currently inhabited by tens of millions of people to annual flooding. Without a sharp reduction in greenhouse gas emissions, this may increase to hundreds of millions in the latter decades of the century.[[30]](#cite_note-Sea_level_rise_Kulp-2019-30)

Local factors such as [tidal range](/wiki/Tidal_range "Tidal range") or land [subsidence](/wiki/Subsidence "Subsidence") will greatly affect the severity of impacts. For instance, [sea level rise in the United States](/wiki/Sea_level_rise_in_the_United_States "Sea level rise in the United States") is likely to be two to three times greater than the global average by the end of the century.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-Sea_level_rise_oceanservice.noaa.gov-32) Yet, of the twenty countries with the greatest exposure to sea level rise, twelve are in Asia, including [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") and the Philippines.[[33]](#cite_note-Sea_level_rise_Shaw-33) The resilience and [adaptive capacity](/wiki/Climate_change_adaptation "Climate change adaptation") of ecosystems and countries also varies, which will result in more or less pronounced impacts.[[34]](#cite_note-34) The greatest [impact on human populations](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change_on_humans "Effects of climate change on humans") in the near term will occur in low-lying [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") and [Pacific islands](/wiki/Pacific_islands "Pacific islands") including [atolls](/wiki/Atolls "Atolls"). Sea level rise will make many of them uninhabitable later this century.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Societies can adapt to sea level rise in multiple ways. [Managed retreat](/wiki/Managed_retreat "Managed retreat"), [accommodating coastal change](/wiki/Coastal_management "Coastal management"), or protecting against sea level rise through hard-construction practices such as [seawalls](/wiki/Seawall "Seawall")[[36]](#cite_note-36) are hard approaches. There are also soft approaches such as [dune rehabilitation](/wiki/Sand_dune_stabilization "Sand dune stabilization") and [beach nourishment](/wiki/Beach_nourishment "Beach nourishment"). Sometimes these adaptation strategies go hand in hand. At other times choices must be made among different strategies.[[37]](#cite_note-Sea_level_rise_Thomsen-2012-37) Poorer nations may also struggle to implement the same approaches to adapt to sea level rise as richer states.

## Aviation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sea_level&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Aviation")]

Main article: [Altitude in aviation](/wiki/Altitude_in_aviation "Altitude in aviation")

Pilots can estimate height above sea level with an [altimeter](/wiki/Altimeter "Altimeter") set to a defined [barometric pressure](/wiki/Barometric_pressure "Barometric pressure"). Generally, the pressure used to set the altimeter is the barometric pressure that would exist at MSL in the region being flown over. This pressure is referred to as either [QNH](/wiki/QNH "QNH") or "altimeter" and is transmitted to the pilot by radio from [air traffic control](/wiki/Air_traffic_control "Air traffic control") (ATC) or an [automatic terminal information service](/wiki/Automatic_terminal_information_service "Automatic terminal information service") (ATIS). Since the terrain elevation is also referenced to MSL, the pilot can estimate height above ground by subtracting the terrain altitude from the altimeter reading. [Aviation charts](/wiki/Aviation_chart "Aviation chart") are divided into boxes and the maximum terrain altitude from MSL in each box is clearly indicated. Once above the transition altitude, the altimeter is set to the [international standard atmosphere](/wiki/International_standard_atmosphere "International standard atmosphere") (ISA) pressure at MSL which is 1013.25 hPa or 29.92 inHg.[[38]](#cite_note-38)
